Black Footed Albatross.description: A large seabird with a wingspan up to 2.2 m known for graceful soaring. Mates for life and can live over 60 years.

Laysan Albatross.description: Famous for elaborate courtship dances and remarkable navigational abilities spanning thousands of miles.

Red Winged Blackbird.description: One of North America's most abundant birds. Males have striking red and yellow shoulder patches.

Brewer Blackbird.description: A medium-sized blackbird with iridescent plumage. Highly adaptable and often found in human-disturbed environments.

Yellow Headed Blackbird.description: Unmistakable with its brilliant yellow head. Males produce a distinctive harsh buzzing song.

Bobolink.description: Travels up to 20,000 km annually. The only North American land bird with reverse plumage — black below, white above.

Indigo Bunting.description: A brilliant azure blue songbird. The vivid blue is structural, not pigment-based. Males navigate by the stars.

Painted Bunting.description: Often called the most beautiful North American bird. Males are a mosaic of red, blue, and green.

Cardinal.description: Iconic bird of eastern North America. Both male and female sing — a rarity among North American songbirds.

Barn Swallow.description: The world's most widely distributed swallow. Distinctive forked tail used for acrobatic aerial flight.

American Crow.description: One of the most intelligent birds, capable of using tools and recognising human faces.

Fish Crow.description: Nearly identical to the American Crow. Distinguished by its distinctive 'uh-uh' nasal call.

Belted Kingfisher.description: One of the few bird species where females are more colourful than males. Digs burrows up to 1.5 m into earthen banks.

Anna Hummingbird.description: Males produce a distinctive buzzing with tail feathers during dive displays. Overwinters in North America.

Ruby Throated Hummingbird.description: The only hummingbird breeding in eastern North America. Migrates non-stop 800+ km across the Gulf of Mexico.

Rufous Hummingbird.description: One of the most aggressive hummingbirds. Makes one of the longest migrations relative to body size.

American Goldfinch.description: State bird of three US states. Moults twice per year trading bright yellow plumage for drab olive-brown in winter.

Herring Gull.description: One of the most familiar large gulls. Known to drop shellfish on hard surfaces to crack them open.

Brown Pelican.description: The only dark pelican and the only one that plunge-dives from height. A conservation success story after DDT ban.

White Pelican.habitat: Large inland lakes, marshes, coastal bays
White Pelican.description: One of the largest North American birds with wingspan up to 2.9 m. Herds fish cooperatively in groups.

Mockingbird.description: Can mimic up to 200 other bird species. Males may sing through the night during breeding season.

Puffin.habitat: Open North Atlantic, breeds on sea cliffs
Puffin.description: Known as the 'clown of the sea'. Can carry up to 10 fish crosswise in its bill simultaneously.

Rhinoceros Auklet.description: Named for a horn-like protrusion during breeding season. More closely related to puffins than true auklets.

Cedar Waxwing.description: Passes berries beak-to-beak down a line of birds. Sometimes intoxicated from eating fermented berries.

Western Meadowlark.description: State bird of six western US states. Its flute-like song is iconic on western prairies.

Horned Lark.description: The only true lark native to North America. Named for tiny black feather tufts ('horns') on males.

Yellow Billed Cuckoo.description: One of the few birds that readily eats hairy caterpillars. Called the 'Rain Crow' for calling before thunderstorms.

Tropical Kingbird.habitat: Open areas, forest edges, urban areas
Tropical Kingbird.description: The most widespread kingbird in the Americas. Aggressively defends territory against much larger birds.

Purple Martin.habitat: Open areas near water, urban areas
Purple Martin.description: The largest North American swallow. East of the Rockies, almost entirely dependent on human-provided nest boxes.

Horned Grebe.habitat: Lakes, ponds, coastal waters in winter
Horned Grebe.description: Distinguished by spectacular golden 'horn' feather tufts during breeding season.

American Pipit.description: Distinctive for its bobbing tail motion while walking. Breeds on arctic and alpine tundra.
